result:
The most astonishing thing I ever learned is that everyone is the main character of their own story. The barista who hands you your coffee is, in her mind, not “the barista,” but a person whose thoughts are dominated by a looming student loan payment, the novel she’s trying to write, and the quiet joy of a text from a new love. The man impatiently tapping his foot in the elevator isn’t just an obstacle; he’s rushing because he’s visiting his father in the hospital, his heart a clenched fist of worry.

We move through the world surrounded by these epic, intricate narratives, but we only ever read the cover. We interact with caricatures—the slow driver, the cheerful cashier, the stern boss—when each is a universe of memory, hope, and private grief. Their lives have climaxes and tragedies, mundane chapters and thrilling twists, all invisible to us.

This isn’t a call for sentimental pity, but for a quiet, radical awareness. It makes the world infinitely more interesting and complex. That moment of frustration in a crowded queue is suddenly populated not by anonymous bodies, but by a chorus of inner monologues. It fosters a gentler patience, a curiosity that replaces snap judgment. To remember this is to step out of the relentless spotlight of your own concerns, if only for a second, and acknowledge the profound, humming tapestry of other consciousnesses. It is the ultimate cure for loneliness and the surest antidote to arrogance. We are all living our epic, in real time, together.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
